exemplar.title: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

detailedExemplarData.title: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

strengths: Sustained, clear focus on the prescribed title throughout the essay
Effective linkage to two areas of knowledge (History and the Human Sciences) with concrete illustrative examples
Coherent, well-structured arguments supported by specific real-world evidence (e.g., female labour in the Industrial Revolution; Structural Adjustment Programmes; Finlandâs UBI pilot)
Demonstrates awareness of multiple perspectives (patriarchal vs feminist historians; IMF orthodoxy vs local economists)
weaknesses: Predominantly descriptive treatment of the knowledge question, with limited conceptual unpacking of key terms such as âcontradictory evidenceâ and âethical responsibilityâ
Evaluation of different points of view is minimal and counterclaims are not systematically examined
Implications of accepting or rejecting contradictory evidence are acknowledged only superficially
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examiner awarded an overall mark of 8, indicating performance in the 7â8 band for Understanding Knowledge Questions. Although the essayâs treatment of the knowledge question remains at times descriptive and its critical evaluation could be deeper, it nonetheless maintains a sustained focus on the title, integrates two areas of knowledge effectively, and supports its arguments with well-chosen examples. The acknowledgment of multiple perspectives and coherent structure justify elevating the essay into the high band. Assigning the full 8 marks to this single gradable criterion reflects the total mark provided by the examiner and aligns with the demonstrated strengths while noting areas for further conceptual development.

annotations.comment: The use of Mary Paulâs diary and factory records as concrete evidence demonstrates engagement with primary sources, grounding the historical example in realâworld data.
The student immediately aligns the essay with the prescribed title and explicitly identifies both History and the Human Sciences, establishing clear focus on the required areas of knowledge.

Key terms such as âcontradictory evidenceâ and âethical obligationâ are invoked but not defined; the essay would benefit from an early, precise characterization of these concepts to guide analysis.
There is no explicit formulation of a knowledge question; the student should present a clear KQ (e.g. âTo what extent do historians have an ethical duty...?â) to scaffold the essayâs inquiry.
The introduction effectively links the directive to broader ethical concernsâbias, misinformation and public trustâhighlighting the relevance of the KQ across both fields.
Consider reflecting on potential limitations of the selected examples and proposing related questions for further inquiry to deepen critical engagement.
This discussion remains largely descriptiveâthere is minimal analysis of how inclusion of marginalized voices reshapes the historianâs methodological approach or ethical stance.
The essay refers to âbiasâ and âtransforming effectâ but does not unpack how feminist historians justify the reliability of oral histories compared to traditional archives.
The contrast between patriarchal and feminist perspectives is described, yet the student stops short of evaluating the epistemic implications of each framework on historical knowledge.
The chronological progression from 19th-century narratives to 20th-century feminist critiques effectively maps the development of historical methodology over time.
Citing feminist historians like Rowbotham and Taylor shows awareness of contrasting viewpoints and the evolution of historiographical methods to include previously excluded evidence.
The statement on historiansâ ethical duty is asserted but not linked to concrete criteria for evaluating when evidence has been unjustly excluded, limiting conceptual depth.
The SAP discussion would benefit from connecting selective data use to specific ethical frameworksâcurrently it remains at the level of policy critique without epistemic analysis.
Introducing principles of rigour, transparency and objectivity in human sciences anchors the discussion in recognized ethical standards for empirical research.
The IMF and World Bank case is descriptive; the student should analyse how selective data use reflects specific methodological choices and ethical breaches in evidence appraisal.
There is an opportunity to contrast quantitative vs qualitative evidence in social science methodologies, but this methodological reflection is missing.
Referencing Stiglitzâs critique connects the SAP example to established scholarly debates, demonstrating use of credible counter-evidence against dominant economic narratives.
The transition to UBI feels abrupt and underdeveloped; the student should clarify how this case directly illustrates the perils of ignoring contradictory evidence.
The analysis conflates ethical obligations of knowledge producers with policy outcomes; the student should separate epistemic responsibilities from normative policy judgments.
The comparison of qualitative well-being metrics versus quantitative sustainability concerns in the UBI trial illustrates recognition of methodological complexity in human sciences.
The discussion of UBI lacks a clear definition of what counts as âcontradictory evidenceâ here; the student should specify criteria for including or excluding data.
The second paragraph reiterates earlier points without critically evaluating new evidence; more depth is needed on longâterm methodological limitations of the pilot.
The conclusion succinctly restates the essayâs focus and synthesizes examples from both AOKs, reinforcing the overarching ethical imperative.
The conclusion remains largely descriptive; to reach higher levels, it should evaluate the implications of embracing contradictory evidence for future knowledge production.
There is scope to discuss how sample size and selection bias in the Finnish trial affect evidence reliability, but this methodological reflection is absent.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
